The step up in Europe is where the numbers cool — his eight Champions League appearances in 2025 returned just 6.5, and the 2024 Europa League run sat at 6.7, a reminder that the toughest nights have stretched him.

The international picture is kinder. He posted 7.22 at the Euros, 7.07 through World Cup qualifying, and his two 2026 friendly outings touched 7.3. For a Spain side ranked second in the world, that profile suggests a goalkeeper who is reliable behind a dominant team but not a one-man rescue act. If Spain control games, his ratings hold up; if they get opened up, the club data hints the margin is thinner than the ranking implies.

J. Pickford.text: Pickford arrives at 31 as the settled custodian behind a side ranked fourth by FIFA, and his club record reads like a metronome: an ever-present 38 Premier League appearances in both 2024 and 2025, 3,420 then 3,432 minutes, with no rotation to speak of. The durability matters for a tournament where a first-choice keeper can play seven games inside a month. What gives slight pause is the trend in the numbers. His Everton league rating eased from 7.26 in 2024 to 7.02 in 2025, and his most recent international work has dipped further — 6.90 across two World Cup qualifiers and 6.75 over two friendlies in 2026. That is a soft decline rather than a collapse, and small samples in qualifying carry their own noise, but it nudges his level back toward the 6.8-7.0 band rather than the 7.2-plus peaks of his cup outings. For a deep England run, steady is the realistic expectation, not spectacular.

Y. Bounou.text:
Yassine Bounou heads to the tournament as a settled, in-form number one. His Al-Hilal league work has been steady rather than spectacular — 6.82 across 31 Pro League games in 2024, nudging up to 6.93 over 26 appearances in 2025 — but the pattern that matters here is how he raises his level on the big stage. He posted 7.42 in the AFC Champions League Elite in 2024 and a striking 7.78 across five FIFA Club World Cup outings in 2025, exactly the high-stakes, knockout environment a World Cup demands.

The Morocco numbers reinforce it: 7.17 over seven games at the Africa Cup of Nations and 7.33 in three 2026 friendlies. At 34 he is not declining, and a goalkeeper who consistently lifts his rating when the games get bigger is a quiet asset for a side ranked eighth in the world. For a team that may need to win matches by margins, that kind of reliability behind them is worth plenty.

M. Maignan.text:
Maignan arrives as the settled number one for the world's top-ranked side, and his club record reads like a metronome. Two near-identical Serie A campaigns frame him neatly: 37 appearances and 3,295 minutes at a 7.1 average in 2024, then 37 appearances and 3,303 minutes at a sharper 7.23 in 2025. That is a goalkeeper who plays nearly every week and is trending mildly upward, with his Champions League work (7.22 across ten games) and a standout 7.7 in the Coppa Italia underlining that the level holds against better opposition.

The mild caveat is the France shirt. His tournament football for the national side has been strong — 7.33 at the Euros, 7.34 in the Nations League — but the recent international samples are softer, a 6.62 in World Cup qualifying and 6.83 across three 2026 friendlies. For a France team modelled as a genuine contender, his club consistency is the reassuring signal; the dip in recent national-team ratings is the small open question worth watching.

Alisson Becker.text:
Alisson's club numbers tell a story of gentle decline from a very high base. His 2024 was his strongest run, a 7.11 average across 28 Premier League games and a standout 7.62 over six Champions League nights. The 2025 league campaign softened that picture: 26 appearances at 6.78, his weakest sustained rating in the dossier, even as his European form held firmer at 7.18. The encouraging signal for Brazil is that he has rediscovered something on international duty, posting 7.18 across nine World Cup qualifiers — comfortably above his recent Liverpool league baseline and right back at his 2024 Copa America level.

For a 33-year-old goalkeeper, that mix of a slightly dipping club floor and a solid qualifying ceiling reads as steady rather than commanding. Brazil sit sixth in the FIFA ranking, and Alisson remains a dependable rather than spectacular last line — enough to keep his side competitive, short of the form that single-handedly wins knockout ties.

Diogo Costa.text:
Costa is Porto's undisputed number one and the trend is gently upward. Across 2024 he logged 32 Primeira Liga starts at a 6.98 rating, with a slightly softer 6.89 in the Europa League. The 2025 season tightened that picture: 33 league appearances and 2,907 minutes at 7.07, plus 7.05 in Europe and a tidy 7.33 across his Taça de Portugal outings. There's no drama in these numbers, which for a goalkeeper is exactly the point — he plays nearly every minute and his form holds steady rather than spiking or sagging.

The international sample flatters him further. He posted 7.34 at the Euros and 7.23 in the Nations League, both comfortably above his club baseline, even if a 6.60 across six World Cup qualifiers is a reminder that the floor exists. For a team ranked fifth in the world, that combination — durable, rarely below 7, and demonstrably capable of raising his level on the biggest stage — is close to ideal heading into the tournament.

T. Courtois.text:
At 33, Thibaut Courtois remains Real Madrid's first-choice and the numbers say the level has held. Across 2024 he averaged 7.11 over 32 La Liga starts and lifted that to 7.43 in twelve Champions League outings — knockout football, where his ratings tend to climb rather than wobble. The 2025 campaign tells a slightly softer domestic story, his La Liga mark easing to 6.93 across 32 appearances, but he answered with a 7.47 at the FIFA Club World Cup and a 7.29 in eleven Champions League matches, the bigger stage again drawing the better goalkeeper.

For Belgium, ranked ninth, that pattern is the encouraging read going into 2026. The two friendlies so far returned a steady 7.05, and a keeper who consistently raises his game in elite knockout ties is exactly the profile a side leaning on its defence wants behind it. The domestic dip is worth noting, but nothing here suggests a goalkeeper in decline.

T. Hayakawa.text:
Hayakawa's club trajectory is a steady upward line. At Kashima he has been an ever-present in the J1 League, a full 38 appearances and 3,420 minutes in both 2024 and 2025, and his match rating has climbed with the workload — from 7.06 in 2024 to 7.15 in 2025 and 7.38 across 17 games so far in 2026. For a 26-year-old goalkeeper, that's a profile heading the right way at the right time, with the consistency of minutes that selectors tend to trust.

The international picture is thinner. His Japan exposure has come in fragments — an EAFF outing rated 7.23, a couple of friendlies, and most recently an eight-minute cameo. For a side ranked 18th in the world, that suggests Hayakawa is in the conversation rather than nailed-on as first choice. The club form makes a strong case; the small sample of caps means the tournament likely opens with him as cover rather than the certain starter, unless the rising numbers force the issue.

O. Baumann.text:
At 35, Oliver Baumann arrives as the kind of dependable, low-drama goalkeeper a tournament squad leans on rather than builds around. His Hoffenheim workload tells the story: a near ever-present 28 Bundesliga appearances in 2024 at a 7.01 rating, then a full 34-game shift across 2025, where his mark dipped slightly to 6.81. That is steady rather than spectacular, and the trend points gently downward rather than up.

The concern sits in the most recent data. Across three Germany friendlies in 2026 he has averaged just 6.27, his weakest stretch in this sample and a step below the 6.92 he posted over six World Cup qualifiers in 2024. For a side ranked tenth in the world, that softening late form is worth watching. Baumann offers experience and consistency between the posts, but nothing here suggests a goalkeeper rounding into peak shape at exactly the wrong age, and the friendly numbers leave his starting claim genuinely open.

preview.text:
Home advantage doesn't get much more emphatic than the Azteca, and the numbers frame this as a clear mismatch. Mexico arrive 15th in the world and worth €191.85m to South Africa's €49.25m, riding a run capped by a 5-1 friendly win over Serbia, while Bafana have stumbled through draws with Nicaragua and Jamaica and a March loss to Panama. The model reads it accordingly: 2.06 expected goals for the hosts against just 0.56, a 72.2% Mexico win and only 8.6% for South Africa. Mexico are favoured to open the scoring 72.5% of the time, and the cleanest outcomes on the board are 2-0 (15.4%) and 1-0 (14.5%), with both teams scoring rated a modest 38.1%.

Where the model and the market part company is on the margins of certainty. Polymarket has Mexico priced at a flat 100 cents — effectively no chance for the draw or an upset — whereas the model still leaves almost 28% with South Africa avoiding defeat. On goals the gap flips: the market sits firmly under 2.5, while the model has over 2.5 at a near coin-toss 48.8%. So treat the winner as well-signposted but not sealed, and the total as a genuinely open question rather than the formality the market implies.

review.text:
Mexico opened their World Cup at the Estadio Azteca with a controlled 2-0 win over South Africa, the kind of result the numbers had been pointing at all along. It was a clean continuation of the home side's recent form, and the scoreline landed exactly on the model's single most likely outcome: 2-0 sat top of the projected scores at 15.4 percent, just ahead of 1-0. A 2.06 expected-goals figure for Mexico against 0.56 for South Africa described this game well in advance, and the shutout chimed with a below-even both-teams-to-score read of 38 percent.

This was a good night for both the model and the market, with little to separate them. The model priced Mexico at 72.2 percent to win; Polymarket had them at 69.5 cents — close agreement that South Africa, ranked 60th to Mexico's 15th, would struggle to find a foothold. The locked bracket pick of 1-0 Mexico nailed the result if not the exact margin, and a first-to-score read of 72.5 percent for the hosts captured how the game was always likely to flow. A straightforward call, and a correct one.

preview.text:
South Korea host the Czech Republic in Guadalajara as modest favourites in a matchup the model reads as close. The projected goals — 1.46 to 0.98 — tilt Korean, and that feeds a 47.4% home win against 24.9% for the visitors, with the draw a live 27.7%. The reasoning is partly venue and partly that Korea, ranked 25th to the Czechs' 41st, carry the edge despite trailing on squad value (€139m to €188m). Both sides arrive in form, Korea off back-to-back friendly wins, the Czechs unbeaten across their last nine, which helps explain why the model sees a tight, low-event game: over 2.5 goals lands at just 44%, both teams to score at 48.6%, and the likeliest scores are 1-1 (13.1%) and 1-0 (12.1%).

The gap with the market is stark rather than marginal. Polymarket prices this as a certainty across the board — home win at 100 cents, over 2.5 goals at 100, both teams to score at 100 — where the model treats every one of those as closer to a coin flip or worse. That is not confirmation of either view; it is a genuine disagreement. The model's numbers say this is a contest the Czechs can win or draw, and the market's all-or-nothing pricing leaves no room for that.

review.text:
South Korea opened their Group A campaign with a 2-1 win over the Czech Republic in Guadalajara, turning a tight contest into the kind of narrow home victory the numbers leaned toward without ever promising. The Czechs, the more valuable squad on paper at €188m to Korea's €139m and unbeaten in their previous nine before kickoff, got on the scoresheet but couldn't take a point from the Estadio Akron.

Grade this one a clean hit for the model. It made South Korea favorites at 47.4% to win, with the draw at 27.7% and the Czechs trailing on 24.9%, and the result landed squarely on the right side. The exact 2-1 scoreline was the fifth-likeliest line at 9.1%, and with an xG read of 1.46 to 0.98 the model expected Korea to edge the goal count without a blowout — which is exactly what happened. BTTS came in too, just ahead of the model's near-coin-flip 48.6%. Our locked bracket pick, a 1-1 that called the home result correctly, also graded as a hit. A solid, unflashy night for the projections.

preview.text:
Canada open their home World Cup at BMO Field as heavy favourites, and the model leans into it: 2.36 expected goals to Bosnia's 0.57, a 76.97% home win, and a forecast that Canada strike first 75.8% of the time. The most likely scorelines are all clean sheets — 2-0 at 14.8%, 1-0 at 12.2%, 3-0 at 11.6% — which tells you how the numbers read this: a hosting side with the ranking edge (30th to 65th) and the squad value (€198.65m to €151.6m) controlling territory against opponents the model expects to struggle to score. BTTS sits at just 39.9%.

The disagreement with the market is the real story. Polymarket prices Canada at only 53.5 cents to win, with the draw at 27.5 and Bosnia as live as 20.5 — far above the model's 6.92% for an away win. The market clearly respects Bosnia's qualifying pedigree, including those results against Italy and Wales, in a way the model does not. The two also part on goals: the model has over 2.5 at 56.1% against the market's 42.5. So treat this as genuinely open at the margins — the model sees a comfortable, possibly emphatic Canadian win; the market sees a contest Bosnia can disrupt.

The United States open Group D at home in Los Angeles as clear favourites, and the model leans into it: an expected 1.58 goals to Paraguay's 0.82, a 54.6% win probability against 26.4% for the draw and 18.9% for Paraguay. The shape of the matchup explains the gap — a squad valued at €385.65m against Paraguay's €153.65m, a side ranked 16th hosting one ranked 40th, and the memory of a 2-1 US win when these two met as recently as last November. The most likely scoreline is a tight 1-0 at 13.7%, with 1-1 close behind at 12.4%, so this reads as a controlled home win rather than a rout: the US are 59.4% to score first but only 43% of games here clear 2.5 goals.

Where it gets interesting is the moneyline. The market is more cautious about the hosts, pricing them at 47.5 cents against the model's 54.6%, and crediting Paraguay at 24.5 cents versus the model's 18.9%. That is a genuine disagreement worth respecting — the US friendly form is patchy, with losses to Germany, Portugal and Belgium among recent home games. On totals and both teams to score the two sides essentially agree, so the open question is purely how much resistance Paraguay offer.

This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it as one. Switzerland, ranked 19th and carrying a squad valued at €332.5m against Qatar's €19.93m, are made 80.9% favourites, with the draw at 13.1% and Qatar held to just 6.1%. The expected-goals split tells the same story: 2.78 for the Swiss, 0.67 for Qatar, a gap that flows naturally from recent form. Qatar arrive without a goal in their last two friendlies and have lost four of their last ten, while Switzerland have won five of theirs and put four past both Jordan and Kosovo. The model has the Swiss favoured to score first 77.8% of the time and to lead at the break 60.6%, with 0-2 (12.3%) and 0-3 (11.4%) its likeliest scorelines.

On the result the market barely budges from the model — Polymarket's 80.5% on Switzerland and 13.5% draw are near-identical. The honest disagreement is on goals. The model sees over 2.5 landing 66.9% of the time and both teams scoring at 45.9%; the market is more cautious at 57.5% and 37.5%. That leaves a genuine open question about how freely this game flows, even if nobody doubts who wins it.

This is a heavyweight group-stage opener that the model reads as far tighter than the matchup might suggest. Brazil sit sixth in the world to Morocco's eighth, and carry nearly double the squad value at €928.2m against €498.3m, but the expected-goals split is modest: just 1.06 to 0.82 in Brazil's favour. That feeds a cagey set of probabilities — Brazil home at 39.9%, the draw a hefty 33.1% and Morocco still live at 27.0% — with 0-0 actually the single most likely scoreline at 16.0%. Morocco arrive on a serious run, with an AFCON campaign full of clean sheets behind them, and the model clearly respects their organisation: both teams to score is priced at only 37.2%.

Where the model and the market part ways is on Brazil's standing. Polymarket has the favourites at 59.5 cents, almost twenty points clear of the model's read, and trims Morocco to 16.5 against the model's 27.0. The totals tell the same story: the market sees over 2.5 goals at 46.5%, the model far lower at 29.0%. So this is a genuine open question — the market backs Brazilian class to tell, the model expects a low-scoring, closely fought night, and one of them will be graded wrong.

Scotland arrive in Boston as the heavier name — ranked 43rd to Haiti's 83rd, with a squad valued at €170m against €56m — and the model treats them as favourites without ever quite running away with it. The expected goals split 1.58 to 1.19 in Scotland's favour, which translates to a 45.9% away win, a 28.1% Haiti win and a stubborn 26.1% draw. Scotland are also marginally more likely to open the scoring at 53.2%, and the single most probable scoreline is a 1-1 draw at 12.4%, with low-scoring Scotland wins close behind. Form backs the lean: Scotland have won four of their last five including 4-0 at Bolivia, while Haiti mix a 4-0 over New Zealand with defeats to Peru and Tunisia.

This is one of those genuine open questions where the model and the market part ways. Polymarket prices Scotland at 62 cents, far bolder than the model's 46%, and shades Haiti down to just 16 against the model's 28. The totals are barely worth arguing about — both have over 2.5 around the coin-flip mark, 52% to 50 cents — but the books clearly see a more comfortable Scottish night than the numbers do. The model says Haiti are live; the market says they are not.

This is a tight one on paper. The model gives Australia a narrow home edge at 37.7 percent, with Turkey at 33.4 and the draw a live outcome at 29 percent, and the expected goals back that up: 1.24 to 1.15, barely a sliver between them. Turkey's recent form is the heavier read — wins over Romania and Kosovo in qualifying, a draw away to Spain, and a squad valued at €473.7m against Australia's €77.45m — but the model leans on the home venue at BC Place and Australia's own steady run to keep this close to a coin flip. The goal market is quiet: over 2.5 lands at 42.9 percent, both teams to score at 49.3, and the most likely scoreline is a 1-1 at 13.7 percent, with 1-0 and 0-1 close behind.

The real argument is on the result line. Polymarket reads this very differently, pricing Turkey at 57.5 cents and pushing Australia down to just 18.5 — a gap of nearly twenty points on the home side versus the model's 37.7. The totals are barely contested (over 2.5 at 46.5 cents against the model's 42.9), so all the tension sits in who wins. Treat that as a genuinely open question, not a settled one.

This is a mismatch dressed up as a fixture. Germany, ranked 10th and carrying a €947m squad, arrive on the back of seven straight wins, against a Curaçao side ranked 82nd whose €25.78m squad has shipped four to Scotland and five to Australia in recent friendlies. The model reads it accordingly: 3.5 expected goals for Germany against just 0.55, a 89.95% home win, and an 84.78% chance Germany open the scoring. The likeliest outcomes are all comfortable home margins — 3-0 at 12.45%, 4-0 at 10.9%, 2-0 at 10.67% — and over 2.5 goals lands at 76.93%. The shape is straightforward: territory, possession and pressure flowing one way.

Where the model and market part company is in degree, not direction. Polymarket prices Germany even shorter at 94 cents to the model's 90, and leans harder into goals, putting over 2.5 at 82.5% against the model's 77%. The sharper split is at the other end: the model gives Curaçao a 41.2% chance of scoring at some point, where the market sees only 32.5%. So both agree Germany should win at a canter — the open question is whether Curaçao manage to register on the scoreboard at all.

On paper this is a mismatch — Netherlands rank seventh in the world and carry a squad valued at €754.2m against Japan's €270.85m — but the model sees a coin-flip leaning faintly the wrong way. Its expected goals split barely separates the sides, 1.26 for Japan against 1.21 for the Netherlands, and that nudges the moneyline toward Japan at 36.9% with the Dutch on 34.6% and the draw at 28.5%. The reasoning is in the recent results: Japan arrive on a long unbeaten thread including wins over England, Scotland and Brazil, while the Netherlands have looked uneven, losing 0-1 to Algeria and drawing with Ecuador in recent friendlies. The most likely scoreline is 1-1 at 13.5%, with a tight, low-event game the theme — over 2.5 goals is priced at just 44.9%.

This is where the model and the market part company sharply, and it is a genuine open question rather than a settled one. Polymarket makes the Netherlands clear favourites at 48.5 cents and prices Japan all the way down at 25.5, almost the mirror image of the model's read. The two broadly agree on the texture of the game — both see over 2.5 around the coin-flip and both-teams-to-score near 51-53% — but they disagree fundamentally on who wins it. Trust reputation or trust recent form.

Ecuador arrive in Philadelphia as the marginally better-ranked side (23rd to Ivory Coast's 34th) and the model treats this as a low-scoring, finely balanced affair rather than a mismatch. Projected goals tell the story: 0.83 expected for Ecuador against 0.69 for the Ivorians, two cagey teams whose recent form is built on tight margins — Ecuador's last 10 are littered with draws (1-1 against the Netherlands, Morocco, the USA, plus a famous 1-0 over Argentina), while Ivory Coast warmed up by beating France 2-1. The result is a genuine three-way moneyline: draw at 37.6%, Ecuador 35.5%, Ivory Coast 26.97%, with a goalless 0-0 the single most likely scoreline at 22.5%.

This is a low-event game in the model's eyes, and that's exactly where it parts company with the market. The model sees over 2.5 goals at just 19.6% and both teams scoring at 28.7%; Polymarket prices those far higher, at 31.5 and 42 cents respectively. The two agree closely on the result — the market has Ecuador at 39.5 cents, the draw at 33.5 — so the real disagreement is about goals, not the winner. Backing the under and the no on BTTS is where the model stakes its ground.

Sweden open as narrow favourites in Monterrey, and the model frames this as a tight, low-scoring game rather than a mismatch. The expected goals split is modest at 1.22 to 0.96, which feeds a moneyline of 41.4 percent for Sweden, 30.2 percent for the draw and 28.4 percent for Tunisia — closer than the gap in squad value, where Sweden's 406 million euros dwarfs Tunisia's 70 million, might suggest. The scoreline distribution tells the same story: 1-1 leads at 13.9 percent, with 1-0 and 0-0 close behind, and the model leans under, pricing over 2.5 goals at just 37.4 percent and both teams to score at 44.4 percent. Half-time most likely ends level at 47.9 percent.

The clearest disagreement is with the market. Polymarket has Sweden at 50.5 cents, a full nine points above the model's 41.4 percent, and trims both the draw and Tunisia accordingly. The market also expects a slightly more open game, with over 2.5 at 43.5 cents against the model's 37.4 percent and BTTS at 48. So treat this as a genuine open question: the model sees a coin-flip tinged in Sweden's favour, while the market is more convinced the Swedes simply win.

This is a mismatch on every axis the model can measure: world number two against number 69, a €1.22bn squad against one worth €54.5m, and a goal expectation of 2.98 to 0.41. Spain arrive having won by 3-0 or more repeatedly through qualifying, and the model reads it accordingly — an 88.1% home win, with the most likely scorelines clustered tightly around 2-0 (15.0%), 3-0 (14.9%) and 4-0 (11.1%). Cape Verde are not a soft touch on recent form, with wins over Serbia and Cameroon on the board, but the model gives them just a 2.65% chance and only an 11.6% shot at scoring first. Both teams to score sits at 31.8%, a nod to Cape Verde's threat rather than a real expectation of it.

The market leans a touch more emphatically toward Spain: Polymarket prices the home win at 91.5 cents against the model's 88.1, and trims the draw to 6.6 cents where the model sees 9.3. The gap is small but consistent — the market also nudges over 2.5 goals up to 70.5% versus the model's 65.7%. In short, both agree on the destination; the only live question is whether Spain's margin is as crushing as the price implies, and the model is fractionally more cautious.

Belgium arrive as clear favourites and the model frames this as a class gap as much as a form one: ninth-ranked and carrying a squad valued at €547.5m against Egypt's €116.48m, they project to 1.5 expected goals to Egypt's 0.71. That asymmetry runs through every market. Belgium win 55.65% of simulations, the draw lands at 27.36% and Egypt at just 16.99%, and they are favoured to open the scoring 60% of the time. The most likely scoreline is a tidy 1-0 (16%), with 2-0 and 1-1 close behind, the picture of a side expected to control without necessarily running up the score against an Egypt defence that kept clean sheets against Spain and at the recent Africa Cup of Nations.

The interesting friction is over goals rather than the result. The model sees a cagey, low-scoring affair: over 2.5 goals at 37.8% and both teams scoring at just 39.88%. Polymarket leans noticeably busier, pricing over 2.5 at 48.5 cents and both-teams-to-score at 48.5 — a meaningful gap that treats this as a more open game than the numbers do. On the result the two broadly agree, with the market's 59.5 cents on Belgium only fractionally bolder than the model.

This is a clear mismatch on paper, and the model treats it that way. Uruguay, ranked 17th to Saudi Arabia's 61st and carrying a squad valued at €359.3m against €40.68m, project to 1.57 expected goals to the Saudis' 0.43. That feeds a 65.6% Uruguay win, with the draw at 25.2% and Saudi Arabia given just 9.3%. The shape is one of containment versus pressure: Uruguay are favoured to open the scoring 67.7% of the time, and the single likeliest outcome is a tidy 0-1 at 20.8%, with 0-2 close behind at 16.7%. Goals should be scarce — both teams to score sits at only 28.1%, and over 2.5 lands under a third of the time at 32.4%.

The market broadly agrees on the headline but reads the game as a livelier one. Polymarket has Uruguay at 66.5 cents, almost identical to the model, but prices the home side a touch higher at 11.5 and trims the draw to 21.5. The wider gap is on goals: the market's 46.5 cents on over 2.5 and 41.5 on both teams scoring sit well above the model's 32.4% and 28.1%. That tension over how open this becomes is the genuine open question here.

Iran arrive ranked 21st in the world against a New Zealand side sitting 85th, and the model leans accordingly without ever turning the matchup into a mismatch. The projected scoreline of 1.46 to 1.01 in expected goals points to a contest Iran should control but not run away with, which is why their win sits at just 46.9% with the draw a live 27.6% and New Zealand still handed 25.5%. The form lines tell a consistent story: Iran beat Mali 2-0 and Gambia 3-1 in their last two friendlies, while New Zealand have lost six of their last ten, including a 0-4 thrashing by Haiti and a 0-1 to England this month. The likeliest scores are tight — 1-1 at 13.1%, 1-0 at 11.7%, with a goalless draw still carrying 9.1%.

The clearest disagreement is on the favourite's price. Polymarket has Iran at 52.5 cents to the model's 46.9%, and shrinks New Zealand to 19.5 against the model's 25.5% — the market is markedly more convinced Iran win and more dismissive of an upset. The two sides are closer on goals, both seeing a low-scoring affair: the model's 44.9% for over 2.5 edges the market's 39.5, and they almost agree on both teams scoring at 49.5 against 44.

France arrive top of the FIFA ranking with a squad valued at €1,520m against Senegal's €478m, and the model reads this as a clear but not crushing edge. It expects 1.42 goals from France and 0.86 from Senegal, which translates to a 49.6% home win, a 28.3% draw and a 22.0% chance Senegal take it. The two also part ways on goals: the model leans under, pricing over 2.5 at 39.8%, while the market sits near a coin-flip at 49.5 cents. So the genuine open question is whether France are as dominant as the betting suggests, or whether Senegal's quality keeps this tighter and lower-scoring than the favourites' price implies.

This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it that way. So the direction is agreed — Norway, comfortably — but the market expects a more emphatic, higher-scoring win than the numbers do. That gap is the genuine open question here.

A heavy favourite meeting a side that travels well: that's the shape here. Argentina, ranked third and carrying a squad valued at €782.5m against Algeria's €256.9m, have won eight of their last ten, while Algeria arrive on the back of a 1-0 win in the Netherlands and a run of clean sheets through the Africa Cup of Nations. That feeds a 64% home win, a 23.8% draw and only a 12.2% Algeria upset, and the favoured scorelines are tight ones — 1-0 (16%) and 2-0 (14.3%) lead the board.

The market sees the same winner but trusts it more, pricing Argentina at 70.5 cents against the model's 64%, and trimming the draw to 20.5. The sharper split is on goals: Polymarket has over 2.5 at 48.5 cents while the model sits at 41.8%, and the cheaper-end totals lean the same way. So the honest disagreement is about volume, not outcome — the market expects a slightly more open, higher-scoring afternoon than the model, which leans toward a low-scoring Argentina hold. BTTS is close, 38.4% to 41.

Austria arrive as clear favourites, ranked 24th to Jordan's 63rd and carrying a squad valued at €242.2m against €20.3m, and the model reads the matchup the obvious way: a side that has won eight of its last ten driving play against opponents who lost their two most recent friendlies, 0-2 to Colombia and 1-4 to Switzerland. The expected-goals split, 1.95 to 0.78, frames it as control rather than a shootout, and the projected scorelines bear that out — 2-0 (12.4%), 1-0 (12.3%) and 1-1 (10.4%) top the board. That last figure is where the disagreement lives. Polymarket prices Austria at 73.5 cents, nearly nine points clear of the model, and trims the draw to 16.5% and Jordan to 9.5% against the model's 13.6%. The market is simply more convinced this is routine; the model leaves a fatter tail for an upset or a stalemate. The two lines agree closely on goals — over 2.5 sits at 51.4% to the market's 57.5%, and both put both-teams-to-score around 46% — so the open question is margin, not entertainment. Jordan's Arab Cup run, including a 3-0 win at Egypt, argues they are not pushovers.

This is a clear favourite against a stubborn underdog. Portugal sit fifth in the world to DR Congo's 46th, and the squad-value gap is vast — €1,010m against €143.9m — so the model frames it as a question of whether Portugal can break down a side that defends well rather than whether they win. Portugal are 67.8% to score first and the model gives them a 65.8% win, leaning on DR Congo's recent miserliness — a 0-0 with Denmark, plus shutout wins over Jamaica and Cameroon.

The disagreement is one of degree, not direction. Polymarket prices Portugal at 76.5 cents to win against the model's 65.8%, treating this as closer to a formality. The same split runs through the goals markets: the model sees over 2.5 at just 37.5% while the market sits at 56.5%, and BTTS at 32.9% against 41 cents. In short, the market expects a more open, higher-scoring afternoon than the numbers do — worth watching if DR Congo's defensive record holds.

England arrive in Dallas as the heavy favourite, and the model leaves little doubt why. This is a low-event projection: over 2.5 goals lands at just 39.6 percent and both teams to score at 40.1 percent, consistent with a side that wins by suffocation. The market broadly agrees on the favourite but trims the edges, pricing England at 56.5 cents and giving Croatia a slightly warmer 18.5 against the model's 15.5. The clearer disagreement is on goals: Polymarket has both teams to score at 47.5 cents and over 2.5 at 44.5, noticeably above the model. Whether Croatia's attack can puncture England's defence is the open question here.

Despite sitting 41 places below Panama in the FIFA rankings, Ghana open this Group L tie as home favourites at BMO Field, and the squad-value gap explains why: their players are worth €234.6m against Panama's €34.55m. This shapes up as a tight, modestly-scoring contest: the most likely scorelines are 1-1 (13.5%), 1-0 (11.8%) and a goalless draw (9.8%), and the model splits 0-0 to over-three-goal outcomes almost evenly, with over 2.5 priced at just 42.7%.

There is no real argument with Polymarket here. The market has Ghana at 44.5 cents, the draw at 27.5 and Panama at 27.5, a near-mirror of the model's read, and the totals agree just as tightly — over 2.5 at 41.5 against the model's 42.7%, both-teams-to-score at 48 against 48.4%. When model and market line up this cleanly, the honest call is that the price is fair.

This is a clear favourite-against-outsider setup at the Azteca, and the numbers reflect it. Colombia sit 13th in the world to Uzbekistan's 50th, with a squad valued at €302m against €85m, and the model hands them a 1.5 expected-goals edge over the Uzbeks' 0.63. That translates to a 57.98% win probability for Colombia, 27.11% for the draw and just 14.91% for Uzbekistan. The shape is low-scoring rather than a rout: under 2.5 goals is the likelier outcome at 64%, both teams scoring is only a 36.88% shot, and the model's four most probable scorelines are all tight — 0-1, 0-2, 0-0 and 1-1. Colombia are first to score 61.78% of the time, but a goalless first half (46.89%) is the single most likely halftime state.

Where the model and market part company is on degree. Polymarket prices Colombia at 70.5 cents to win, well clear of the model's 58%, and trims Uzbekistan to 9.5 and the draw to 20.5. The market also expects more goals, pricing over 2.5 at 49 cents against the model's 35.95%. So the disagreement isn't about who wins — it's whether Colombia win comfortably and openly, or grind through a cagey, tight afternoon. Treat that as genuinely open.

The Czech Republic open Group A in Atlanta as clear favourites, and the numbers explain why. Their squad is valued at €188m against South Africa's €49m, they sit 19 places higher in the FIFA ranking, and they arrive on a strong run — five wins in their last six, including draws held against Denmark and Croatia. The model reflects that edge without overstating it: 1.32 expected goals to 0.85, a 47.2% win probability against South Africa's 23.3%, with the draw a live 29.6%. This shapes up as a controlled rather than emphatic favourite's afternoon. The Czechs are likeliest to draw first blood (53.6%), but at half-time the model actually favours parity at 47.7%, hinting at a slow build rather than an early breakaway.

That points to a tight, low-scoring game: under 2.5 goals is the bet, with over 2.5 only 36.9% and 1-0, 1-1 and 0-0 the three most probable scorelines. Here the market pushes back hardest. Polymarket prices the Czechs at 57.5 cents to South Africa's 17.5, a notably bolder favourite than the model's, and leans to more goals — over 2.5 at 44.5 cents against the model's 36.9%. On the win line and the total, then, the market sees a clearer, busier Czech victory than the model will endorse.

Switzerland are the clear favourites at SoFi Stadium, and the gap is built on more than the rankings — 19th against 65th, a squad valued at €332.5m against €151.6m, and a model expecting them to carry the play. The numbers point to a controlled Swiss performance: 2.06 expected goals to Bosnia's 0.62, a 71.2% chance of scoring first, and a moneyline reading of 70.6% home. That tilt comes off a strong Swiss qualifying run, with four-goal wins over Kosovo and Sweden, while Bosnia arrive unbeaten in their last competitive run but draw-heavy, with stalemates against Macedonia and Panama in their warm-ups. The model's top scorelines — 2-0 at 14.5%, 1-0 at 13.6% — suggest a tight, low-scoring win rather than a rout, with over 2.5 goals essentially a coin flip at 50.3%.

Where the model and market part ways is on how alive this is for Bosnia. Polymarket prices the away side at 16.5 cents and the draw at 23.5, against the model's leaner 9.7% and 19.7%. In other words, traders see meaningfully more upset and stalemate risk than the goal model does. That disagreement is worth respecting: Bosnia's recent results say they are awkward to break down, so treat the Swiss favouritism as strong but not settled.

This is a heavy mismatch on paper, and the model reads it that way. Canada, ranked 30th to Qatar's 55th and carrying a squad valued near ten times higher (€198.65m to €19.93m), get an expected 3.18 goals at BC Place against just 0.61. Where the numbers part ways is on degree, not direction. Polymarket has Canada at 74.5 cents, a clear step below the model's 86%, and inflates the draw to 17.5 against the model's 9.8%. Mexico get this group A opener at home in Guadalajara, and the model treats them as clear but not overwhelming favourites: 1.53 expected goals to South Korea's 0.97, which translates to a 49.7% win probability against 23.4% for the visitors and a 26.9% draw. Mexico are favoured to open the scoring at 55.8%, and the most likely scoreline is a tight 1-1 at 12.8%, just ahead of 1-0.

The interesting gap is on goals. The model sees a livelier game: 71.9% for over 1.5 against the market's 65.5%, and 45.7% for over 2.5 where Polymarket sits at 40%. Both-teams-to-score is close at 49.3% to 46.5%. If you believe the model, the value is in the over.

On home soil at Lumen Field, the model frames this as a clear but not commanding United States edge: 1.51 expected goals to Australia's 1.05, which feeds a 47.4% home win, a 27.0% draw and a 25.6% Socceroos upset. The lean rests on the obvious gap in resources — a €385.65m squad against €77.45m, FIFA's 16 against 27 — and on recent history, with the USA having beaten Australia 1-2 in Sydney last October. The scoreline distribution is tight and low-scoring: 1-1 tops the board at 12.9%, ahead of 1-0 at 11.1%, with both teams to score essentially a coin flip at 51.2% and over 2.5 priced at 47.2%.

The interesting friction is on the moneyline. In short, the market backs the hosts harder than our numbers do, treating Australia's chances as thinner than the model will allow. On totals the two agree almost exactly — over 2.5 at 46.5 cents against 47.2% — so the genuine open question here is purely how much credit the Socceroos deserve, not how many goals get scored.

Morocco arrive in Boston as the clear class side, ranked 8th in the world against Scotland's 43rd, with a squad valued at €498m to €170m. The model reflects that gap bluntly: Morocco are 65.8% to win, Scotland just 10.1%, with the draw at 24.1%. Scotland's 4-0 friendly win over Bolivia flatters a team that lost to both Japan and Ivory Coast in March.
