The market also leans higher-scoring: both teams to score sits at 47.5 cents versus the model's 32.4%, and over 2.5 goals at 40.5 against 36.9%. So the shape is agreed — Morocco on top — but how comfortably, and how open, is a genuine open question.

This is as lopsided as group-stage football gets. Brazil, ranked 6th with a squad valued at €928m, host a Haiti side ranked 83rd and worth €55.9m, and the model reflects that gulf: it projects 3.35 goals for Brazil against 0.58, and makes them 88.3% to win with the draw a distant 8.5% and a Haiti upset just 3.2%. The scoreline distribution is a parade of clean-sheet wins — 3-0 (12.3%), 2-0 (11%) and 4-0 (10.3%) head the list — and Brazil are 83.4% to open the scoring. There is decent over-3.5 value too, priced at 55.3% by the model, with over-2.5 a comfortable 75.2%.

Where it gets interesting is at the edges. The market is firmer on Brazil than we are, pricing them at 90.5 cents to our 88.3, and shaving the draw to 6.1 cents. The bigger gap is both teams to score: the model sees 42.7%, the market only 35.5% — a genuine disagreement worth flagging given Haiti's recent form, which includes a 4-0 win over New Zealand and a 3-3 draw at Costa Rica. They can find the net, even if keeping Brazil out looks beyond them.

Turkey come in ranked 22nd and on a strong run, eight wins in their last ten including qualifying victories over Romania and Kosovo and a 2-2 draw away to Spain, while Paraguay sit 40th with a more mixed record of friendly results either side of solid CONMEBOL qualifying. That gap, plus the squad-value chasm of €473.7m to €153.65m, is why the model leans Turkey but only narrowly: home win 40.4%, draw 30.4%, Paraguay 29.2%. The expected goals tell the same cautious story, 1.2 to 0.98, pointing to a tight, low-scoring game rather than a mismatch. The single most likely score is 1-1 at 14.0%, with 1-0 and 0-0 close behind, and over 2.5 goals is only a 37.1% shot.

Where model and market part ways is on the margins. Polymarket prices Turkey a touch higher at 43.5 cents and trims Paraguay to 26.5, so it sees the favourite as the clearer side; the model keeps Paraguay live as a genuine third of the time. The market also expects more goals, pricing both teams to score at 49.5 cents against the model's 44.2% and over 2.5 at 41 to 37. Treat the favourite's edge and the goal count as the real open questions here.

The shape here is a clear favourite against a side arriving in poor form. The Netherlands sit seventh in the world to Sweden's 38th, with a squad valued at €754.2m against €406.1m, and the model leans heavily on that gap: it projects 2.1 expected goals for the hosts to just 0.96 for Sweden, and makes the Dutch 63.1% to win in Houston. Form backs the read. The Netherlands lost only once in their last ten and battered Finland, Malta and Lithuania 4-0 in qualifying, while Sweden have collapsed badly, taking only a couple of recent wins amid heavy defeats to Switzerland (1-4 and 0-2) and Kosovo. The model has the Netherlands 65.1% to score first and 2-0 as the single likeliest scoreline at 10.3%.

The market broadly agrees but trims the edges. Polymarket prices the Dutch at 59.5 cents to the model's 63.1%, nudging the draw and Sweden slightly longer in the model's eyes. The sharper disagreement is on goals: the model sees Over 2.5 at 59% against the market's 51.5 cents, and both-teams-to-score at 54.6% versus 50.5. So the genuine open question isn't who wins but how open it gets — the model expects more goals than the market is willing to pay for.

Germany arrive in Toronto on a near-flawless run, nine wins in their last ten with the only blemish a 0-2 loss in Slovakia, and a squad valued at €947m against Ivory Coast's €522m. But the gap is narrower than the FIFA rankings (10th versus 34th) suggest: the Ivorians have won eight of ten themselves, including a 2-1 away win over France this June. The model reads it as a clear but far from settled favourite's game, projecting 1.42 expected goals for Germany against 0.97 — enough for a 46.7% win probability, with the draw live at 28.1% and Ivory Coast holding a real 25.2% puncher's chance. The likeliest scorelines are tight: 1-1 at 13.3%, 1-0 at 12.4%, and a goalless draw still 9.8%.

This is where the model and the market part company sharply. Polymarket prices Germany at 63.5 cents, treating the win as close to a formality, while the model's 46.7% says it is anything but. The market also leans higher-scoring, pricing over 2.5 goals at 52.5 cents against the model's 42.8%. The two only truly agree on both teams scoring, near-identical at 48 cents and 47.7%. Treat Germany's margin, not the result, as the genuine open question.

This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it as one. Ecuador sit 23rd in FIFA's ranking to Curaçao's 82nd, with a squad valued at €368.7m against €25.78m, and the goal model hands the favourites a 2.06 expected-goals edge to just 0.39. That works out to a 76.7% win probability, a clean sheet in most outcomes, and a 76.5% chance Ecuador score first. The form lines back the gap: Ecuador are unbeaten across their last ten, including a 1-0 win over Argentina, while Curaçao have shipped four to Scotland and five to Australia in recent friendlies. The most likely scorelines are tightly bunched around a low-scoring home win — 2-0 at 18.3%, 1-0 at 17.5%, 3-0 at 12.6% — and both teams to score is only a 28.4% shot.

Market and model largely agree on the winner: Polymarket prices Ecuador at 80.5 cents to the model's 76.7%, a modest lean toward the favourite. The clearer disagreement is on goals. The market sees this game as livelier, pricing over 2.5 at 57 cents while the model has it at just 44.2%, and BTTS at 35.5 cents against the model's 28.4%. If you trust the numbers, the value sits with the unders and a tighter, lower-scoring afternoon in Kansas City.

This is a clear mismatch on paper, and the model treats it like one. Japan arrive ranked 18th with a squad valued at €270.85m against Tunisia's €69.95m, and recent form sharpens the picture: Japan have beaten England, Scotland and Iceland 1-0 apiece, while Tunisia were taken apart 0-5 by Belgium and lost 0-1 to Austria in their last two warm-ups. The model projects 1.61 expected goals for Japan against just 0.56 for Tunisia, and makes Japan 62.6% to win with the draw at 25.3% and a Tunisia win down at 12.1%. The single likeliest scoreline is 0-1 at 17.9%, followed by 0-2 at 14.8%, and Japan are 65.3% to open the scoring.

The market broadly agrees but shades a touch more cautious on Japan, pricing them at 58.5 cents to the model's 62.6%, and nudging Tunisia up to 15.5 cents. The sharper gap is on goals: Polymarket sees both teams scoring at 45.5%, well clear of the model's 34.7%, and leans slightly over on totals, with over 2.5 at 41.5 cents versus the model's 36.8%. That leaves a genuine question over whether Tunisia find the net at all — the model expects a tighter, lower-scoring Japan win than the market is bracing for.

This is about as lopsided as group-stage fixtures get. Spain arrive ranked second in the world with a squad valued at €1.22bn against Saudi Arabia's €40.68m, and the form lines underline the gap: Spain are unbeaten across their last ten with qualifying wins like 6-0 in Türkiye and 4-0 in Georgia, while Saudi Arabia have lost five of their last ten, including a 0-4 home defeat to Egypt. The model reads it accordingly, handing Spain an expected 2.84 goals to 0.4 and an 86.9% win probability against just 2.9% for the Saudis, with a draw a remote 10.2%. Spain are 84.1% to open the scoring, and the scoreline distribution clusters tightly around clean sheets — 2-0, 3-0, 1-0 and 4-0 are the four likeliest results, which is why both-teams-to-score sits at only 31%.

There is almost nothing to argue about here, and the market agrees. Polymarket prices Spain at 87.5 cents to the model's 86.9%, with the draw and the Saudi win line up similarly close. The totals match too: over 2.5 goals is 62.7% in the model against 65 cents in the market, and over 1.5 lands at 83.5% versus 85.5. Model and market are effectively shaking hands.

Belgium arrive in Los Angeles as comfortable favourites, and the shape of this one is the classic mismatch of resources: a squad valued at €547.5m against Iran's €32.05m, ninth in the world against twenty-first. The model leans hard into that, projecting 1.8 expected goals for Belgium against 0.83 for Iran and pricing the home win at 60%, the draw at 24% and an Iran upset at just 16%. Belgium have scored freely in the build-up — 5-0 over Tunisia, 2-0 in Croatia — and the model makes them 63% to open the scoring. The most likely scorelines all favour them, with 1-0 (12.5%) and 2-0 (11.7%) leading the board.

This is not expected to be a goal feast, though. The model sits almost exactly on the fence for over 2.5 goals at 48.8%, and both teams to score is a coin-flip at 47.5%, reflecting Iran's capacity to defend deep and frustrate. The market is a touch more bullish on Belgium, pricing the home win at 68.5 cents against the model's 60% and trimming Iran to 11.5 cents. That gap is the one genuine disagreement worth flagging: the model gives Iran a little more daylight than the traders do.

Uruguay arrive ranked 17th with a squad valued near €359m against Cape Verde's €55m, and the model reads the gap exactly as a mismatch of class against organisation. Expected goals split 1.65 to 0.44, which is why the home side prices at 67% to win, against a 24% draw and just under 9% for Cape Verde. Uruguay are tipped to open the scoring 69% of the time, and the most likely scorelines are tight and one-sided — 1-0 at 20%, 2-0 at 17%, with a goalless draw still third on the list at 13%. This is a control job rather than a shootout: Cape Verde have kept clean sheets in qualifying but conceded in patches, and Uruguay's recent friendlies have been low-scoring and cagey.

The market broadly agrees on the result — Polymarket has Uruguay at 69.5 cents, close to the model's read — but the two pull apart on goals. The model sees over 2.5 at just 35%; the market splits it 50/50. Both-teams-to-score tells the same story, 29% from the model against 45% priced in. So the honest open question here isn't who wins, but whether Cape Verde find the net at all — and the model is markedly more sceptical than the traders.

Egypt arrive in Vancouver as clear favourites on paper — ranked 29th to New Zealand's 85th, with a squad valued at €116.48m against the All Whites' €34.35m — but this profiles as a tight, low-scoring affair rather than a procession. The model gives Egypt a 0.84-1.25 expected-goals edge and a 45.2% win chance, against 24.3% for New Zealand and a hefty 30.5% for the draw. That caution is everywhere in the scoreline: over 2.5 goals lands at just 34.7%, both teams scoring at 41.2%, and the three likeliest results are 0-1, 1-1 and 0-0. New Zealand's form does little to argue otherwise, with eight defeats in their last ten and recent maulings by Haiti and Australia.

Where it gets interesting is the gap to Polymarket. The market is firmly more bullish on Egypt, pricing them at 54.5 cents to the model's 45.2%, and trims the draw to 25.5 cents against the model's 30.5%. The market also leans slightly higher on goals, with over 2.5 at 42 cents versus the model's 34.7%. So the genuine open question is whether Egypt's quality simply overruns a fragile New Zealand, as the market expects, or whether this stays the cagey, draw-friendly contest the model sees.

Argentina arrive ranked third in the world with a squad valued at €782.5m against Austria's €242.2m, and the model treats this as exactly the mismatch the numbers suggest. It hands the hosts a 1.88 to 0.51 expected-goals edge and a 70.1% win probability, with Austria down at 8.8% and the draw at 21.2%. Argentina are favoured to open the scoring 71.1% of the time, and the most likely outcomes are all clean sheets — 1-0 at 16.8%, 2-0 at 16.2%, 3-0 at 10.2% — so this projects as a controlled, low-event win rather than a shootout. Tellingly, both teams to score sits at just 34.3%, reflecting how little the model expects from an Austrian side that has nonetheless quietly won eight of its last ten.

The market is more cautious about the favourite. Polymarket prices Argentina at 61.5 cents against the model's 70%, and gives Austria nearly double the model's chance, 16.5 cents to 8.8%. The same split shows on both-teams-to-score, 47.5 cents versus 34.3%, suggesting traders rate Austria's defensive names and recent form more highly than the goals model does. The totals barely differ — over 2.5 sits at 42.7% to the market's 48% — so the genuine open question is simply how much resistance Austria can muster.

This is a heavyweight mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. France, ranked first in the world with a squad valued at €1.52bn against Iraq's €21.2m, are projected for 2.51 expected goals to Iraq's 0.40, and that gap drives a moneyline of 83.2% for the home win against just 3.9% for Iraq, with the draw at 13%. The scoreline distribution is stacked with clean sheets: 2-0 (17.2%), 3-0 (14.4%) and 1-0 (13.5%) head the list, France are 81.4% to score first, and both teams to score sits at only 30.4%. Iraq arrive in decent form — a 1-1 draw at Spain and a play-off win over Bolivia — but the numbers say their job here is damage limitation.

The market broadly agrees but leans heavier in two places. Polymarket prices France at 87.5 cents to win, a touch firmer than the model's 83.2%, and trims the draw to 9.5. The clearer split is on goals: the market has over 2.5 at 65 cents and over 3.5 at 41.5, against the model's 55.5% and 33.2%. So the open question isn't who wins, but how many France pour in — and there the market expects a heavier French scoreline than the model does.

This is the matchup the model can't separate. Senegal arrive ranked 14th in the world to Norway's 31st, and the expected goals back that edge ever so slightly: 1.21 for Senegal against 1.13 for Norway, a margin small enough that the moneyline splits almost evenly — 37.2 percent for Senegal, 33.4 for Norway, with a thick 29.4 percent draw reflecting how tight this projects. Norway come in on a strong run, including 4-1 wins over Italy and Estonia in qualifying, while Senegal's recent record is more mixed, an AFCON campaign followed by a 3-2 friendly loss to the USA. The model reads this as a coin-flip leaning marginally to the more highly ranked side, with low scoring: only 41 percent of outcomes clear 2.5 goals, and 1-1 tops the scoreline chart at 13.8 percent.

The market sees it very differently, and the gap is the story. Polymarket prices Norway as clear favourites at 45 cents, with Senegal down at just 28.5 — almost the mirror image of the model, which makes Senegal the slight pick. The market also expects more goals, pricing both teams to score at 55.5 percent against the model's 48.2. That's a genuine open question worth watching, not a settled one.

This is a clear mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. Algeria sit 28th in the world to Jordan's 63rd, carry a squad valued at €256.9m against €20.3m, and arrive off a 1-0 win away to the Netherlands. The numbers reflect that gap: 2.36 expected goals for Algeria against just 0.72 for Jordan, a 73.7% win probability, and a 72.9% chance of striking first. The likeliest scorelines all run through an Algerian win — 0-2 (12.8%), 0-1 (10.5%) and 0-3 (10.1%) head the board — and Jordan's recent form, including a 1-4 loss to Switzerland and a 0-2 defeat to Colombia, does little to argue otherwise.

The market broadly agrees but is more cautious about the favourite. Polymarket prices Algeria at 65.5 cents to the model's 73.7%, while giving Jordan (14.5c) and the draw (20.5c) more room than the model's 9.2% and 17.2%. The other notable split is on goals: the model sees over 2.5 as a near-coin-flip at 59.4%, where the market sits at 48.5%, leaning under. On both questions the disagreement is real rather than decisive — the model expects a comfortable, fairly open Algeria win; the market hedges slightly toward caution and a tighter scoreline.

This is a heavyweight against a clear underdog: Portugal sit fifth in the world with a squad valued at €1.01bn, Uzbekistan fiftieth and worth €85m. The model frames it accordingly, projecting 1.8 expected goals for Portugal against just 0.58, and making them 66% to win with the draw a live 23% and an Uzbek upset only 11%. The shape is a Portugal side expected to control and score first 68% of the time, but not to run riot — the single most likely scoreline is a tight 1-0 at 16%, with 2-0 close behind, and a goalless draw still carries a 10% tag. Both teams scoring is below even money at 37%.

Where the model and the market part ways is on conviction. Polymarket prices Portugal at 79.5 cents to win, fully thirteen points clear of the model's read, and trims the draw to 14.5 cents. The market also expects more goals: over 2.5 trades at 61 cents against the model's 43%, and over 1.5 at 82 versus 69%. So the question isn't who wins but by how much. The model sees a businesslike, low-scoring Portugal job; the market is betting on a more emphatic one.

This is a heavy mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. England sit fourth in the world and arrive on a strong run — seven wins in their last ten, including 2-0 qualifying defeats of Serbia and Albania — while Ghana, ranked 74th, have lost five of their last ten and shipped goals freely against European opposition, 5-1 to Austria and 2-1 to Germany. The squad-value gap says the rest: €1,360m against €234.6m. The model projects 2.18 expected goals for England and just 0.45 for Ghana, making the home side 77.3% to win, with 2-0 (17.2%) and 1-0 (15.4%) the likeliest scorelines and England 76.8% to open the scoring.

The market broadly agrees on the result — Polymarket has England at 74.5 cents, draw at 16.5 and Ghana at 10.5 — but it parts company on how the goals fall. The model sees this as a tighter, lower-scoring afternoon: over 2.5 is a near coin-flip at 48.9%, against the market's 56.5, and both-teams-to-score is priced at 42.5 cents versus the model's 32.3%. So the genuine open question here isn't who wins, but whether Ghana lay a glove on the scoreboard at all.

This is a clear favourite-against-underdog matchup, and the numbers line up with the gap in pedigree: Croatia are ranked 11th to Panama's 33rd, and the squad valuations aren't close at €387.3m against €34.55m. The model reads it that way, projecting 2.09 expected goals for Croatia and just 0.75 for Panama, which translates to a 68.2% away win, a 20.1% draw and only an 11.7% Panama win. Croatia are the side expected to set the tempo — they're 69.1% to score first, and the most likely scorelines are all narrow away wins, with 0-2 (12.8%) and 0-1 (11.8%) leading the board. This looks like control rather than a shootout: over 2.5 goals sits at a coin-flip 54%, and both teams to score is below even money at 46.6%.

The model and Polymarket broadly agree, which is worth noting in itself. The market prices Croatia at 63.5 cents to win, a touch shorter than the model's 68.2%, and nudges the draw up to 23.5 cents against the model's 20.1%. The clearest disagreement is on goals: the model's 54% for over 2.5 is meaningfully above the market's 47 cents. If you trust the model, the value is in expecting a slightly higher-scoring afternoon than the market is paying for.

Colombia carry the favourite's profile into Guadalajara: ranked 13th to DR Congo's 46th, a squad valued at €302m against €144m, and a recent run that includes a 6-3 qualifying win over Venezuela and a 4-0 away win at Mexico. The model reads it as a controlled home job rather than a rout, projecting 1.41 expected goals to DR Congo's 0.55 and pricing Colombia at 57.6% to win, with the draw a live 28.5%. That low away xG reflects a Congo side that has been hard to break down — a goalless draw with Denmark, clean sheets against Cameroon and Benin — and the scorelines bear it out: 1-0 is the single most likely result at 19.4%, with 0-0 next at 14.7%.

The market is more emphatic. Polymarket has Colombia at 66.5 cents, nearly nine points clear of the model, and trims the draw to 21.5%, so this is a genuine disagreement about how comfortable the favourite really is. The two sides also split on goals: the model sees just 31.1% for over 2.5, while the market sits at 43 cents, and both-teams-to-score is priced at 38.5 against the model's 32.5%. If you trust the numbers, the value is in a tighter, lower-scoring game than the betting suggests.

Switzerland arrive ranked 19th to Canada's 30th, with a squad valued at €332.5m against €198.65m and a qualifying run that included home wins over Sweden and Kosovo. Yet the model leans the other way. It hands Canada the larger expected-goals share, 1.47 to 1.18, and makes them the more likely winner at 43.2 percent against Switzerland's 29.8, with the draw close behind at 27. Canada are tipped to score first 51.2 percent of the time, and the projected scorelines reflect a tight, low-volume game: 1-1 tops the board at 12.9 percent, ahead of 0-1 and 1-2. The over 2.5 sits right on the coin-flip line at 49.5 percent, and both teams to score at 54.

This is where the model and the market part company sharply. Polymarket installs Switzerland as the favourite at 44.5 cents and pushes Canada out to just 27.5, almost the mirror image of the model's read. Someone is wrong here, and the reputation and ranking gap is doing the work for the market while the model trusts its goal expectancies. Treat it as a genuine open question. The two sides broadly agree on the totals — over 2.5 priced at 43.5, BTTS at 51.5 — so the dispute is purely about who wins a game both expect to be tight.

Lumen Field hosts a Group B opener that looks closer on paper than the wallets suggest. Bosnia carry a squad worth €151.6m against Qatar's €19.93m, and their recent form reads steadier — draws with Italy and Wales in European qualifying, a 3-1 over Romania — while Qatar arrive on a thin run that includes a 0-3 Arab Cup loss to Tunisia and home defeats to Palestine and Zimbabwe. The model frames it as a modest home edge: 1.35 expected goals to 1.07, Bosnia favoured at 42.5% with the draw (28.5%) and a Qatar win (29.0%) almost level behind. This is a low-scoring lean, with under 2.5 the more likely side at 56.5% and 1-1 the single most probable score at 13.5%.

Here is the genuine split. Polymarket prices Bosnia at 58.5 cents and shoves Qatar down to just 16.5 — far more lopsided than the model, which gives Qatar nearly thirteen points more. The market effectively reads this as a comfortable home win; the model reads it as an open game where a draw or a Qatar result is well within range. On totals the two broadly agree — over 2.5 at 43.5% model versus 47 cents, BTTS near 49% versus 46.5 — so the real argument is purely about how dead Qatar's chances are.

This is a heavy mismatch on paper, and the model treats it that way. Morocco arrive ranked 8th in the world with a squad valued at €498.3m against Haiti's €55.9m, and the form lines back the gap: the Atlas Lions are unbeaten across their last ten, with an unbeaten Africa Cup of Nations run including a 3-0 win at Senegal, while Haiti's friendly results have swung from a 4-0 win over New Zealand to a 1-2 home loss to Peru. The Dixon-Coles engine projects 2.27 expected goals for Morocco against just 0.51 for Haiti, which translates to a 77% home win and only a 6.4% chance of a Haitian upset. A clean sheet is the base case — 2-0 is the single likeliest score at 16%, with 1-0 and 3-0 close behind.

The market broadly agrees but shades a touch friendlier to Haiti: Polymarket prices Morocco at 73.5 cents versus the model's 77%, and the draw at 17.5. The sharper split is on goals at both ends — the model puts both teams scoring at 36%, while the market sits at 43.5 cents, a meaningful gap that pivots entirely on whether Haiti can breach a Bounou-led defence that the model expects to keep tight.

Brazil arrive in Miami as heavy favourites and the model sees little reason to argue: a squad valued at €928m against Scotland's €170m, the world's sixth-ranked side against the 43rd, and an expected-goals split of 2.47 to 0.57 that tells the whole story of the matchup. The model gives Brazil a 78.8% chance of winning against just 6.3% for Scotland, with Brazil first to score 77% of the time and leading at the break in well over half of simulations. The most likely outcomes are clean-sheet Brazil wins — 0-2 at 14.6%, 0-3 at 12% and 0-1 at 11.5% lead the scoreline board — and there's a 58.5% chance the game clears 2.5 goals.

The interesting gap is on the moneyline. Polymarket has Brazil at just 66.5 cents, a fair distance below the model's 78.8%, with the draw (19.5 vs 15%) and a Scotland win (14.5 vs 6.3%) both priced more generously than the numbers suggest. That's a genuine disagreement worth flagging rather than resolving: the market is hedging against an upset the model barely entertains. Elsewhere the two are closer — both have both teams scoring around 40-47% and the over 2.5 line hovering near a coin flip.

Mexico arrive at the Azteca as clear favourites against a Czech side that ranks 41st in the world to their 15th, and the model leans hard into the home advantage of a Mexico City crowd: 1.9 expected goals to 0.81. That gap drives a 62.5% Mexico win, with the draw at 22.6% and the Czechs given just 14.9%. Mexico are the more likely team to break the deadlock too, favoured to score first 65% of the time, and the scoreline distribution tells the same story — 0-1, 0-2 and 0-3 all sit among the five most probable results. The Czechs come in unbeaten across their last ten, but those wins came against the likes of Gibraltar, San Marino and Guatemala; Mexico's recent form, including a 5-1 over Serbia, looks sterner.

The market broadly agrees but trims Mexico's edge. Polymarket prices the away win at 54.5 cents against the model's 62.5%, nudging the Czechs up to 19.5 and the draw to 26.5. The bigger split is on goals: the model has Over 2.5 essentially a coin flip at 51%, while the market sits lower at 43 cents, expecting a tighter, lower-scoring game. On both fronts it's a genuine open question rather than a settled one.

South Korea arrive as clear favourites and the model agrees: ranked 25th to South Africa's 60th, with a squad valued at €139m against €49m, they project to 1.58 expected goals to the hosts' 0.68. That gap drives a 58.4% win probability for Korea, against 26.2% for the draw and just 15.4% for South Africa. Korea are tipped to strike first 62% of the time, and the model's likeliest scorelines all lean their way — 0-1 at 15.9%, 0-2 at 13.0%, with a stubborn 0-0 still carrying 11.0%. This reads as a low-volume contest in which the favourite controls territory but doesn't necessarily run riot: over 2.5 goals lands below an even chance at 39.4%, and both teams scoring is only a 39.8% shot, reflecting how rarely the model expects South Africa to find the net.

The market broadly nods along, pricing Korea at 62.5 cents to South Africa's 15.5 — a touch firmer on the favourite than our 58.4%. The clearer disagreement is on goals. Polymarket has both teams to score at 47.5 cents and over 2.5 at 47, versus our 39.8% and 39.4%. The market expects a more open, higher-scoring affair; the model sees South Africa's attack staying quiet.

At MetLife this is a tight, low-scoring affair on paper. The model gives Germany a slim edge — 1.10 expected goals to Ecuador's 0.92 — and the scoreline distribution backs that up, with 1-1, 0-1 and 0-0 bunched together at the top, each landing within a whisker of 14%. That cautious read flows from Ecuador's defensive record: a side that drew 0-0 with Canada and Paraguay and held the Netherlands and Morocco to 1-1 isn't easy to break down, and the model expects a grind. Germany are favoured at 38.9% to Ecuador's 29.2%, but with the draw at 31.8% this is closer to a genuine three-way toss-up than the ranking gap suggests.

That's where the model and the market part ways sharply. Polymarket prices Germany as clear favourites at 55 cents while pushing Ecuador down to 19, a far more lopsided view than the model's near-even split — the most striking disagreement on the card. The two sides also diverge on goals: the model leans under, pricing over 2.5 at just 32.8% and both teams to score at 40.7%, where the market sees a livelier game at 49.5% and 52.5%. Take your pick; the model says tight and open.

This is a mismatch on paper and the numbers don't pretend otherwise. Ivory Coast arrive ranked 34th with a squad valued at €522.1m against Curaçao's €25.78m, and their recent form is the kind that travels: a 2-1 win over France, a 4-0 over South Korea, a 1-0 over Scotland. The model reads it as a comfortable away job, projecting 2.18 expected goals for Ivory Coast against just 0.5 for Curaçao and making them 75.9% to win, with the draw a distant 17.4% and a home win only 6.7%. Ivory Coast are 75.5% to open the scoring, and the single likeliest result is a 0-2 at 16.3%, with 0-1 and 0-3 close behind.

There's very little daylight between the model and Polymarket here, which is itself worth noting — both see the same one-sided contest. The market is fractionally keener on Ivory Coast at 79.5% versus our 75.9%, and a touch higher on goals, pricing over 2.5 at 57 cents against the model's near coin-flip 50.2%. Both agree this is unlikely to be a shootout: BTTS sits at 35.3% on the model and 38.5% in the market. No genuine disagreement, just a shared expectation of a controlled Ivorian win.

Japan come into Dallas as the model's clear favourite, and the form lines explain why. Despite Sweden carrying the more valuable squad on paper — €406m to Japan's €271m — it's Japan riding three straight 1-0 wins over Iceland, England and Scotland, while Sweden have lost five of their last ten and stumbled through European qualifying. The model reads it as a controlled home edge: 1.9 expected goals to 0.84, a 61.95% win probability, and a 64.6% chance of striking first. The likeliest scorelines are tight and Japanese — 1-0 at 11.8%, 2-0 at 11.7% — with both teams scoring rated just under a coin flip at 48.83%.

This is where the market pushes back hard. Polymarket prices Japan at only 47 cents to the model's 62%, and lifts Sweden to 26.5 cents against the model's 15.39% — a wide gap that treats this as far more of an open contest than the numbers suggest. The draw is nudged up too, 27.5 cents versus 22.66%. On totals the two broadly agree, with over 2.5 goals near even (51.68% model, 45.5 cents market). The honest read: the model loves Japan's recent run, the market trusts it noticeably less, and that disagreement is the bet worth watching.

The shape here is a heavy mismatch. The Netherlands arrive ranked 7th against a Tunisia side 44th in the world, and the squad valuations tell the same story: €754m against €69.95m. The model reads it as a clear away job, projecting 1.77 expected goals for the Dutch to Tunisia's 0.64 and pricing the Netherlands at 64% to win, with the draw at 23% and a home win down at just 12%. That's built on form too — the Dutch have lost only twice in their last ten, while Tunisia were beaten 5-0 by Belgium and 1-0 by Austria in their most recent friendlies. The most likely scorelines are all low and Dutch-favoured: 0-1 at 15% and 0-2 at 14%.

On the result, model and market are essentially in lockstep — Polymarket has the Netherlands at 65.5 cents, the draw at 22.5 and Tunisia at 13.5, almost identical to the model. The disagreement is about goals. The market sees this as a coin-flip for over 2.5 (50 cents) where the model has it at 43%, and prices both teams to score at 48.5 cents against the model's 40%. So the open question isn't who wins, but whether Tunisia find the net at all.

Despite the venue tilting toward Turkey's opponent at SoFi Stadium, this is a road game for the United States in name only, and the model treats it as one they should win. It pegs the US as 53 percent favourites against Turkey's 24 percent, with the draw at 23 percent, and builds that from an expected-goals edge of 1.93 to 1.24. The US are first to score 58 percent of the time, and the likeliest single scorelines all favour the visitors: 1-2 at roughly 10 percent, 0-2 near 8 percent, 0-1 close behind. Turkey arrive on a strong run — eight wins in their last ten, including a 2-2 draw away to Spain — but the model sees an American attack that has put up five against Uruguay and three against Senegal as the sharper threat.

The disagreement with the market is stark and worth flagging. Polymarket prices this as a coin toss, 37.5 cents on each side, where the model leans clearly to the US. The two also split on goals: the model has over 2.5 at 61 percent against the market's 50.5. If you trust the numbers, both the result and the total look like value on the same side — but a pick'em market means plenty of sharp money disagrees.

Two evenly-matched, defence-first sides meet at Levi's Stadium, and the model paints this as a tight, low-scoring affair. Total expected goals sit at just 1.78, Australia edging it on xG at 1.01 to Paraguay's 0.77, and that caution shows everywhere in the numbers: over 2.5 goals is only a 26% shot, both teams to score lands at 35%, and the single likeliest outcome is a goalless draw at 18%, with 0-1 close behind at 16%. Australia, ranked 27th to Paraguay's 40th and unbeaten in their last two warm-ups, are made narrow favourites at 40% to win against Paraguay's 26%, with the draw a live 34%.

The sharper story is where the model and the market pull in opposite directions. Polymarket has Paraguay as the home favourite at 45 cents and Australia out at just 26 — almost the mirror image of the model, which prefers the Australians. Someone is wrong here, and with a higher squad value and home billing behind Paraguay against a model leaning on xG and recent form, it's a genuine open question rather than a settled one. The two also split on goals: the market's 49-cent both-teams-to-score and 40-cent over 2.5 expect a livelier game than the model's grind.

Senegal arrive as heavy favourites and the numbers explain why. The model hands them a 1.79 to 0.47 expected-goals edge, the kind of gap you get when a side ranked 14th with a squad valued at €478.1m meets one ranked 57th worth €21.2m. That translates to a 69.3% win probability against just 8.6% for Iraq, with the draw at 22.1%. The shape is a control-and-suffocate job: Senegal are 70.6% to score first, and the likeliest scorelines are all clean sheets — 1-0 at 18.2%, 2-0 at 16.7%, then 3-0 at 10.0%. Iraq's resilience is real, though; they held Spain 1-1 and Saudi Arabia 0-0 recently, and a 0-0 here carries 10.9%.

On the headline price there is essentially no argument: Polymarket has Senegal at 69.5c, almost exactly the model's read. The disagreement lives in the goals markets. The model sees this as a tighter, lower-scoring game than the market does — over 2.5 goals at 39.3% against 48.5c, and both teams to score at just 31.7% versus a punchy 45c. If you trust Iraq's defensive record, the model's caution looks well placed; if Senegal's firepower runs free, the market's appetite for goals will be vindicated. That is the genuine open question.

France arrive as the world's top-ranked side and the model treats this as their game to lose, pricing them at 54.2% to win against 20.6% for Norway and a 25.2% draw. The expected-goals split tells the same story, 1.69 to 0.96, and France are favoured to open the scoring at 59.1%. None of that makes Norway harmless: a squad worth €589.9m has won six of its last ten and put four past Italy in qualifying, and the model still gives them a one-in-five shout. But the shape here is a stronger, deeper France — squad value €1.52bn, ranked first to Norway's 31st — probing a side that may spend long spells defending. A 1-1 is the single likeliest score at 12%, with 0-1 and 0-2 close behind.

The market and the model are reading this almost identically, which is its own kind of signal. Polymarket has France a fraction shorter on the moneyline at 52.5 cents and Norway slightly longer at 22.5, the only real nudge being marginally more faith in the draw. Totals are a near-perfect match — over 2.5 sits at 49.4% on the model and 49 cents on the market — and both lean to roughly coin-flip both-teams-to-score, 50.8% against 53.5 cents. No edge worth chasing; just agreement.

This is a heavyweight against a grinder. Spain arrive ranked second in the world, riding a long unbeaten run built on 4-0 and 6-0 qualifying scorelines, with a squad valued at €1.22bn against Uruguay's €359m. The model reflects that gap without treating it as a foregone conclusion: it gives Spain 1.37 expected goals to Uruguay's 0.7 and makes them 52.3% to win, with the draw a live 29.1% and Uruguay just 18.6%. The shape it expects is a controlled Spanish edge rather than a rout — Uruguay's recent form is a wall of clean sheets and goalless draws (Algeria, Mexico, Chile), and the model leans that way too, with 0-1, 0-0 and 1-1 its three likeliest scores and a 13.2% chance nobody scores at all.

The interesting friction is in the goals markets. The model sees a tight, low-scoring game: over 2.5 at just 34.4% and both teams to score at 38.3%. Polymarket disagrees sharply, pricing over 2.5 at 49 cents and BTTS at 51.5 — essentially coin flips. The market also nudges Spain's win price up to 59.5 against the model's 52.3. So treat the winner as a genuine open question, but note the clear split on how open the game itself will be.

This is about as level as a World Cup group game gets. The model splits it almost perfectly three ways — Cape Verde 32.5%, the draw 33.8%, Saudi Arabia 33.7% — with expected goals of just 0.91 to 0.93 a side, the signature of two evenly matched teams who don't promise many chances. That low-scoring read runs right through the numbers: over 2.5 goals lands at only 27.9%, both teams to score at 36.8%, and the single most likely outcome on the board is a 0-0 at 16.6%, narrowly ahead of 0-1 and 1-1. Cape Verde arrive on the back of 3-0 friendly wins over Bermuda and Serbia; Saudi Arabia's form is choppier, but their 3-0 in Puerto Rico keeps them live.

The clearest gap with the market is on goals. Polymarket prices over 2.5 at 44 cents and both teams to score at 52.5, comfortably above the model's 27.9% and 36.8% — the market expects a more open game than the numbers do. On the result, Polymarket leans faintly toward Cape Verde at 37.5 cents and trims the draw to 28.5, where the model actually rates the stalemate as the most likely single outcome. Treat both as genuinely open.

This is the closest thing to a coin flip Group G offers. The model gives Egypt 34.5%, the draw 32.6% and Iran 32.95%, with projected goals almost identical at 0.99 to 0.96 — a tight, low-event game between sides that defend better than they create. That low ceiling runs through the totals: over 2.5 lands at just 31.2%, both teams to score at 39.7%, and the single most likely scoreline is 0-0 at 14.8%, narrowly ahead of 1-1. Egypt's slim edge rests partly on a squad valued at €116m against Iran's €32m, though Iran sit eight places higher in the FIFA ranking at 21st, which keeps the matchup honest.

The disagreement is on Egypt's price. Polymarket has them at 43.5 cents to win, against the model's 34.5%, and shades Iran out to 26.5 where the model holds 33%. So the market sees Egypt as clear favourites and the model sees three roughly equal outcomes — a genuine open question rather than evidence either way. The market also leans slightly more towards goals, pricing over 2.5 at 35.5 and both teams scoring at 44.5, both above the model. If you trust the numbers, the value is on Iran's chances and on the game staying tight.

A mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. Belgium come in ranked 9th to New Zealand's 85th, with a squad valued at €547.5m against €34.35m, and recent form to match: five wins in their last six including 5-0 over Tunisia and 2-0 at Croatia, while the All Whites have lost eight of their last ten, shipping four to Haiti only weeks ago. The model projects 2.27 expected goals for Belgium and just 0.72 for New Zealand, and turns that into a 72.1% Belgium win, 18.1% draw and only 9.8% for the hosts. The most likely scorelines all flatter the favourite — 0-2 at 13.0%, 0-1 at 11.1%, 0-3 at 9.8% — and Belgium are favoured to open the scoring 71.8% of the time.

There's little daylight between model and market here. Polymarket prices Belgium at 76.5 cents, a touch firmer than the model's 72.1%, while clipping New Zealand to 8.5 cents and the draw to 16. On the goals, agreement is near-total: over 2.5 sits at 57.3% on the model and 56.5 cents on the market, with both-teams-to-score split 46.3% to 44. No real edge to flag — just a heavy favourite both sides price the same way.

Croatia walk into Lincoln Financial Field as clear favourites, and the gap looks structural: a FIFA ranking of 11 against Ghana's 74, a squad valued at €387.3m against €234.6m, and a model expecting 1.6 goals to Ghana's 0.75. The shape is a familiar one — a settled European side controlling possession against opponents who arrive on the back of a rough run of friendlies, having shipped five to Austria and two each to Mexico and Japan in recent months. The model puts Croatia at 57.1% to win, the draw at 26.1% and Ghana at just 16.8%, and it likes them to open the scoring (61.2% first to score). The single most probable outcome is a tidy 1-0, with 2-0 and 1-1 close behind.

This isn't a goalfest in the numbers. Over 2.5 lands at only 41.6% and both teams to score at 42.6%, consistent with Croatia keeping things quiet. The market broadly agrees but leans a touch more emphatic on the favourite, pricing Croatia at 60 cents versus the model's 57. The clearer disagreement is on goals: Polymarket has both-teams-to-score at 48 cents against the model's 42.6%, treating a Ghana reply as more live than we do.

This is a mismatch the model treats as a near-formality. England arrive ranked fourth in the world against a 33rd-ranked Panama, and the squad values tell the same story in starker terms: €1.36bn against €34.55m. The Dixon-Coles engine projects 2.86 expected goals for England and just 0.44 for Panama, which feeds an 86.2% England win, a 10.5% draw and only a 3.3% home win. England are favoured to open the scoring 83.2% of the time and to lead at the break 65.1%. The most likely scorelines are all clean-sheet England wins — 0-2 at 15%, 0-3 at 14.3% — and over 2.5 goals lands at 64.2%, befitting a one-sided contest where the question is margin rather than result.

The market broadly agrees but tempers the certainty. Polymarket has England at 75.5 cents to the model's 86.2%, with the draw fatter at 15.5 cents against 10.5%, and Panama nudged up to 10.5 cents from 3.3% — a meaningful gap on the underdog and the draw. The clearest split is on both teams to score: the model sees 34.1%, the market 44.5%, suggesting backers give Panama more credit to find a goal than the numbers do. Worth watching, but the headline verdict is shared.

Portugal arrive in Miami as the heavier name — fifth in the FIFA ranking to Colombia's 13th, and a squad valued at just over a billion euros against Colombia's €302m — and the model reads the matchup that way without overcommitting. It gives Portugal a 1.36 expected-goals edge to Colombia's 1.05 and makes them favourites at 43.2%, with the draw and a Colombia win almost level behind at 28.5% and 28.4%. This is a close, low-scoring profile: over 2.5 goals lands at just 43.3%, the most likely scoreline is a 1-1 at 13.5%, and Portugal are marginally the side more likely to open it, scoring first 51% of the time. Both teams arrive in form, Colombia unbeaten in their last four friendlies and Portugal beaten only once in their last ten.

The market broadly agrees but leans a touch harder on Portugal, pricing the away win at 46.5 cents against the model's 43.2% — a real if modest gap that frames this as a live question rather than a settled one. The clearer disagreement is on goals at both ends: Polymarket has both teams to score at 53 cents, comfortably above the model's 49%, so the market expects a slightly more open game than the numbers do.

Two evenly matched debutant-tier sides meet in Atlanta, and the model sees a tight, low-event game. The expected goals tell the story: 0.73 for DR Congo, 0.78 for Uzbekistan, virtually nothing between them and not much for either. That pushes the draw to the front of the moneyline at 37.9 percent, ahead of Uzbekistan at 32.5 and DR Congo at 29.5, despite the Congolese carrying the higher FIFA ranking (46 to 50) and the heavier squad, valued at €143.9m against €85.3m. This shapes up as a goalkeeping afternoon: a 0-0 is the single most likely scoreline at 22.8 percent, both teams to score sits at just 28.6 percent, and the model gives only a 19.2 percent chance of more than two and a half goals.

That last number is where the market pushes back hardest. Polymarket prices over 2.5 goals at 40.5 cents, more than double the model's read, and both-teams-to-score at 47.5 against the model's 28.6. The market also leans firmly toward DR Congo at 41 cents, where the model has a coin-flippier three-way. So the genuine disagreement is twofold: the market expects more goals and more from the Congolese. Treat both as open questions rather than settled facts.

Algeria get the nominal home billing in Kansas City and the model leans their way, projecting 1.33 expected goals to Austria's 1.02 and pricing the hosts at 43% to win against 28% for Austria, with 29% draws. The case is built on form: Algeria beat the Netherlands 1-0 in June and ran deep into the Africa Cup of Nations, while their ranking (28th) sits just behind Austria's (24th) and squad values are near-level at €256.9m to €242.2m. This is a tight, low-volume game in the model's eyes — over 2.5 goals lands at just 42%, both teams to score at 48%, and the single likeliest outcome is a 1-1 draw at 14%, with 1-0 and 0-0 close behind.

The disagreement here is stark and worth flagging. Polymarket has it the other way round, making Austria the favourite at 44.5 cents and Algeria the outsider at just 25.5 — almost a mirror image of the model's read. Austria's qualifying run, including a 5-1 friendly win over Ghana and a settled defence around Alaba and Danso, is clearly weighing on the market. On the goals markets the two broadly agree — over 2.5 within a point either side — so the real question is purely who wins, and on that the model and the money genuinely diverge.

This is as lopsided as group J gets. Argentina, ranked third in the world against Jordan's 63rd, carry a squad valued at €782.5m to Jordan's €20.3m, and the model reads the gap accordingly: an expected goals line of 3.32 to 0.36 and a 91.5% Argentina win. Jordan's path is barely a crack of light at 1.67%, the draw 6.83%. The shape is one-directional. Argentina are 87.9% to score first and lead 71.9% of the time at the break, and Jordan's recent form fits the picture — beaten 4-1 by Switzerland and 2-0 by Colombia in their last two friendlies after a strong Arab Cup run. The likeliest scorelines are 0-3, 0-2 and 0-4.

Where there's room for argument is the margin, not the winner. The market is more cautious on Argentina, pricing them at 82.5 cents against the model's 91.5%, and it leans toward a tighter, more open game: it has both teams to score at 39 cents versus the model's 29.18%, and over 2.5 goals at 60.5 against 71.16%. So the disagreement is real but narrow — the model expects Argentina to bury Jordan, the market expects them to win while conceding the odd chance.
